method2.generated_article:
==Volcano and caldera==
Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai is part of the Tonga segment of the Tonga–Kermadec intra-oceanic arc, formed where the Pacific Plate subducts beneath the Indo-Australian Plate.[4] The broader Tonga–Kermadec subduction system extends for about 2,550 km between New Zealand and Tonga.[4] The volcano is mostly submarine, rises more than 1.8 km from the seafloor, and extends about 20 km across the ocean floor.[4]

The summit of the volcano is a large caldera, described as about 5 km in diameter, with only small parts formerly emerging above sea level as the islands of Hunga Tonga and Hunga Haʻapai.[4] Earlier geological work interpreted the two islands as the visible northern and western remnants of a ridge around a large submarine caldera.[2] Samples from Hunga Tonga contained alternating layers of andesitic lava flows, scoria, lapilli, and ash dipping gently away from the inferred caldera centre.[2] High-resolution multibeam sonar surveys later identified a 4 × 2 km, 150 m deep depression at the expected caldera location, interpreted as the result of explosive collapse during an earlier eruption.[2] The eruptive products of Hunga are andesitic, a composition typical of intra-oceanic arc volcanoes.[4]

The area around a rocky shoal about 3 km south of Hunga Tonga was the site of recorded eruptions in 1912 and 1937.[2] An eruption in 2014–2015 built a large tephra cone that connected Hunga Tonga and Hunga Haʻapai into a single island named Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai.[4] Before the 2022 eruption, the joined island covered just over 1 km² and reached a maximum altitude of 149 m.[5] The January 2022 eruption was described as a once-in-1,000-years event for the Hunga caldera and may have reached as high as VEI 5, pending more precise estimates of erupted volume.[5] The 15 January 2022 explosion destroyed most of the joined island, leaving only small fragments of the former Hunga Tonga and Hunga Haʻapai above the ocean surface.[4][5]

==Geography==
Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai was an uninhabited volcanic island in Tonga in the South Pacific, located about 65 km north of Tongatapu and about 30 km south of the submarine volcano Fonuafoʻou.[2][4]  
The island formed part of Hunga volcano, which lies along the Tonga segment of the Tonga–Kermadec intra-oceanic arc, a subduction-zone system created where the Pacific Plate descends beneath the Indo-Australian Plate.[5]  
The volcano is mostly submerged, rises more than 1.8 km from the seafloor, extends about 20 km across, and has a summit caldera about 5 km in diameter.[5]  
Before the 2014–2015 eruption, only two small portions of the caldera rim emerged above sea level as the separate islands of Hunga Tonga and Hunga Haʻapai.[5]  
Eruptive deposits from the 2014–2015 undersea eruption built a new volcanic cone and connected the two islands, creating the combined island known as Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai.[1][4]  
The newly formed island existed from 2015 until 2022 and became a subject of morphological studies because its rapid erosion and landform evolution offered comparisons with similar features observed on Mars.[4][5]  
Before the January 2022 eruption, the joined island covered just over 1 km2 and reached a maximum elevation of 149 m.[4]  
The January 2022 eruption destroyed most of the combined island, leaving only very small fragments at the western and eastern edges of the former landmass.[4]

==Eruptions==
On 15 January 2022, Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai erupted violently from an undersea vent in the South Pacific, producing massive explosions at about 5 p.m. local time.[4] The eruption generated a vertical plume more than 30 km high, with overshooting tops above 55 km, a record height in the satellite era.[5] Surface-pressure analyses estimated the initial explosion released about 10–28 exajoules of energy, probably exceeding the 1991 Mount Pinatubo eruption and possibly approaching the scale of the 1883 Krakatoa eruption.[5]

The eruption was highly explosive because seawater mixed with magma exceeding 1,000 °C in a confined volcanic system.[3] NASA scientists described the event as unlike a standard Surtseyan eruption, because it involved an unusually large amount of water interacting with magma.[3] One proposed explanation is that part of the caldera’s northern rim collapsed, allowing large volumes of seawater to rush into an underground magma chamber.[3] The event has been informally described by some volcanologists as an “ultra Surtseyan” eruption.[3]

The eruption dramatically altered the island and the surrounding submarine volcano.[3] Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai formed part of the rim of the Hunga Caldera, a 5 km-wide submarine caldera atop a volcano rising 1.8 km from the seafloor and extending about 20 km across.[3] After the eruption, the new land created in earlier activity had disappeared, along with large portions of the older islands.[3]

The eruption triggered tsunamis across Tonga, where waves reached as high as 65 ft above sea level, destroyed hundreds of structures, displaced more than 1,500 people, and caused four deaths.[4] Tsunami waves also affected shores around the Pacific, causing two deaths and an oil spill in Peru and damage in Fiji, Hawaii, Chile, California, Japan, Russia, and New Zealand.[4] Subsequent seafloor surveys found fine sandy mud and deep ash ripples as far as 50 km from the volcano, gouged valleys, and large sediment deposits.[4] Tonga’s domestic internet cable was severed during the eruption and later found buried beneath about 100 ft of sediment and ash.[4]

The eruption injected large quantities of volcanic aerosols and water vapor into the atmosphere and increased total stratospheric water content by about 10%.[2] Although scientists initially expected the stratospheric water vapor to contribute to warming, later studies found that sulfate aerosols produced from sulfur dioxide reflected sunlight and caused an overall slight cooling effect.[1] A UCLA-led study concluded that the eruption reduced Southern Hemisphere temperatures by about 0.1 °C and produced a slight net cooling effect on the planet from 2022.[1]

==Footnotes==
Supplementary materials and datasets associated with a 2024 study of Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai are available online, with generated data deposited in the PANGAEA database and Figshare.[1]

The same publication notes that its maps and figures were generated using Adobe Illustrator, Ocean Data View, and Sigmaplot.[1]

Springer Nature’s publisher note states that it remains neutral with regard to jurisdictional claims in published maps and institutional affiliations.[1]

Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai is part of the Tonga segment of the Tonga–Kermadec intra-oceanic arc, formed where the Pacific Plate subducts beneath the Indo-Australian Plate.[4] The broader Tonga–Kermadec subduction system extends for about 2,550 km between New Zealand and Tonga.[4] The volcano is mostly submarine, rises more than 1.8 km from the seafloor, and extends about 20 km across the ocean floor.[4]

The summit of the volcano is a large caldera, described as about 5 km in diameter, with only small parts formerly emerging above sea level as the islands of Hunga Tonga and Hunga Haʻapai.[4] Earlier geological work interpreted the two islands as the visible northern and western remnants of a ridge around a large submarine caldera.[2] Samples from Hunga Tonga contained alternating layers of andesitic lava flows, scoria, lapilli, and ash dipping gently away from the inferred caldera centre.[2] High-resolution multibeam sonar surveys later identified a 4 × 2 km, 150 m deep depression at the expected caldera location, interpreted as the result of explosive collapse during an earlier eruption.[2] The eruptive products of Hunga are andesitic, a composition typical of intra-oceanic arc volcanoes.[4]

The area around a rocky shoal about 3 km south of Hunga Tonga was the site of recorded eruptions in 1912 and 1937.[2] An eruption in 2014–2015 built a large tephra cone that connected Hunga Tonga and Hunga Haʻapai into a single island named Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai.[4] Before the 2022 eruption, the joined island covered just over 1 km² and reached a maximum altitude of 149 m.[5] The January 2022 eruption was described as a once-in-1,000-years event for the Hunga caldera and may have reached as high as VEI 5, pending more precise estimates of erupted volume.[5] The 15 January 2022 explosion destroyed most of the joined island, leaving only small fragments of the former Hunga Tonga and Hunga Haʻapai above the ocean surface.[4][5]

The island formed part of Hunga volcano, which lies along the Tonga segment of the Tonga–Kermadec intra-oceanic arc, a subduction-zone system created where the Pacific Plate descends beneath the Indo-Australian Plate.[5]  
The volcano is mostly submerged, rises more than 1.8 km from the seafloor, extends about 20 km across, and has a summit caldera about 5 km in diameter.[5]

Before the January 2022 eruption, the joined island covered just over 1 km2 and reached a maximum elevation of 149 m.[4]  
The January 2022 eruption destroyed most of the combined island, leaving only very small fragments at the western and eastern edges of the former landmass.[4]

On 15 January 2022, Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai erupted violently from an undersea vent in the South Pacific, producing massive explosions at about 5 p.m. local time.[4] The eruption generated a vertical plume more than 30 km high, with overshooting tops above 55 km, a record height in the satellite era.[5] Surface-pressure analyses estimated the initial explosion released about 10–28 exajoules of energy, probably exceeding the 1991 Mount Pinatubo eruption and possibly approaching the scale of the 1883 Krakatoa eruption.[5]
